question: Observable vs Promise.
answer: An Observable is a lazy stream of values that starts executing only when subscribed. It can emit zero, one, or many values over time and supports cancellation through unsubscribe(). A Promise is eager, starts executing immediately when created, resolves or rejects only once, and cannot be cancelled. Observables also provide powerful RxJS operators such as map, filter, switchMap, and catchError, making them ideal for Angular applications.
tip: Remember: Promise = One value. Observable = Stream of values. Angular's HttpClient returns Observables because they are cancellable and composable.
deep:
What is a Promise?

A Promise represents the result of a single asynchronous operation. It starts executing immediately after creation and eventually becomes either fulfilled or rejected.

const promise = new Promise((resolve) => {
  console.log('Started');
  resolve('Success');
});

promise.then(console.log);

The executor function runs immediately, even if nobody calls then().

What is an Observable?

An Observable is a lazy stream of asynchronous values. Nothing executes until someone subscribes to it.

const observable = new Observable(subscriber => {
  console.log('Started');
  subscriber.next('Hello');
  subscriber.complete();
});

observable.subscribe(console.log);

The code inside the Observable runs only after subscribe() is called.

Lazy vs Eager

// Promise (Eager)
const promise = fetch('/users');
// Request starts immediately

// Observable (Lazy)
const users$ = this.http.get('/users');
// No request yet

users$.subscribe();
// Request starts here

Single vs Multiple Values

PromiseObservable
One valueZero, one, or many values
Completes automaticallyCan emit indefinitely
// Promise
fetch('/users')
  .then(console.log);

// Observable
interval(1000)
  .subscribe(console.log);
// 0,1,2,3,...

Cancellation

One of the biggest advantages of Observables is that they can be cancelled.

const sub = this.http.get('/users')
  .subscribe();

sub.unsubscribe();

Promises cannot be cancelled once started.

Operators

Observables support powerful RxJS operators for transforming and combining streams.

this.search.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(value =>
    this.userService.search(value)
  )
).subscribe();

Promises support simple chaining using then() but do not have an equivalent operator ecosystem.

Observable vs Promise

FeaturePromiseObservable
ExecutionEagerLazy
ValuesOneZero, One, or Many
Cancellation❌ No✅ Yes
OperatorsLimited (.then)Rich RxJS operators
RetryDifficultBuilt-in (retry)
Angular HttpClient❌ No✅ Yes

Real-world Angular Example

Consider a search box.

this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(text =>
    this.http.get(`/users?q=${text}`)
  )
).subscribe();

Every new keystroke cancels the previous HTTP request using switchMap(). With Promises, older requests would continue running, potentially returning stale results.

When to Use What?

  • Promise: One-time async operations like loading a configuration file or requesting browser permissions.
  • Observable: API calls, form value changes, WebSockets, routing events, user interactions, timers, and any stream of data.

Interview Tip

The most common follow-up question is 'Why does Angular HttpClient return an Observable instead of a Promise?' Mention that Observables are lazy, cancellable, support multiple values, and integrate with powerful RxJS operators like switchMap(), making them ideal for reactive applications.

Interview Answer

"A Promise represents a single asynchronous result and starts executing immediately. An Observable is a lazy stream that starts only when subscribed and can emit multiple values over time. Observables are cancellable and provide rich RxJS operators for transforming and combining streams, which is why Angular's HttpClient and forms are built around Observables instead of Promises."

question: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap.
answer:
These are called flattening operators because they transform an Observable that emits Observables (Observable<Observable<T>>) into a single Observable. The difference lies in how they handle multiple inner Observables.

  • switchMap — Cancels the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives. Best for search/autocomplete and route changes.
  • mergeMap — Runs all inner Observables concurrently without cancellation. Best for independent parallel requests.
  • concatMap — Queues requests and executes them one after another, preserving order. Best for sequential operations like saving records.
  • exhaustMap — Ignores new emissions while the current Observable is still running. Best for preventing duplicate actions such as login or payment requests.
tip: This is the **#1 most asked RxJS interview question.** Remember the keywords: switch = Cancel, merge = Parallel, concat = Queue, exhaust = Ignore.
deep:
Why do we need Flattening Operators?

Many RxJS operators return another Observable. This creates an Observable<Observable>, which isn't useful directly. Flattening operators subscribe to the inner Observable and merge its values into a single stream.

Search Input
      ↓
valueChanges
      ↓
switchMap()
      ↓
HTTP Observable
      ↓
Actual Result

1. switchMap()

switchMap() cancels the previous inner Observable whenever a new value arrives and subscribes only to the latest one.

search.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(term =>
    this.http.get(`/users?q=${term}`)
  )
);
User types:
A
An
Ang
Angu
Angular

Previous API Calls
❌ Cancelled
❌ Cancelled
❌ Cancelled
❌ Cancelled
✅ Only Angular executes

Best For:

  • Search autocomplete
  • Route parameter changes
  • Live filtering
  • Cancel stale HTTP requests

2. mergeMap()

mergeMap() subscribes to every inner Observable immediately. All requests run in parallel and complete independently.

from([1,2,3]).pipe(
  mergeMap(id =>
    this.http.get(`/users/${id}`)
  )
);
Request 1 ─────►
Request 2 ─────►
Request 3 ─────►

All run simultaneously

Best For:

  • Independent API calls
  • File uploads
  • Image processing
  • Background tasks

Warning: Results may arrive in a different order than requests were started.

3. concatMap()

concatMap() queues each inner Observable and executes them one after another. The next request starts only after the previous one completes.

from([1,2,3]).pipe(
  concatMap(id =>
    this.http.post('/save', { id })
  )
);
Request 1 ✅
      ↓
Request 2 ✅
      ↓
Request 3 ✅

Best For:

  • Saving records
  • Database updates
  • Payment processing
  • Operations that must preserve order

4. exhaustMap()

exhaustMap() ignores new emissions while the current Observable is still executing.

fromEvent(loginBtn, 'click').pipe(
  exhaustMap(() =>
    this.authService.login()
  )
);
User clicks:
Click
Click
Click
Click

First Request ✅
Other Clicks ❌ Ignored

Best For:

  • Login button
  • Payment submission
  • OTP verification
  • Preventing double-clicks

Comparison

OperatorPrevious RequestNew RequestOrderTypical Use
switchMapCancelledRunsLatest OnlySearch
mergeMapKeeps RunningRunsNot GuaranteedParallel APIs
concatMapCompletes FirstQueuedGuaranteedSaving Data
exhaustMapKeeps RunningIgnoredFirst OnlyLogin/Payment

Real-world Angular Examples

Search Box → switchMap()

valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(term => this.api.search(term))
);

Dashboard Loading → mergeMap()

from(ids).pipe(
  mergeMap(id => this.api.getUser(id))
);

Save Button → concatMap()

from(records).pipe(
  concatMap(record => this.api.save(record))
);

Login Button → exhaustMap()

fromEvent(loginBtn, 'click').pipe(
  exhaustMap(() => this.auth.login())
);

Memory Trick

OperatorKeyword
switchMap🔄 Switch (Cancel Previous)
mergeMap⚡ Parallel (Everything Runs)
concatMap📋 Queue (One by One)
exhaustMap🚫 Ignore (Busy)

Interview Tip

This question is almost guaranteed in Angular interviews. Interviewers rarely want the definitions alone—they want the correct use case. Mention:

  • switchMap → Search/autocomplete.
  • mergeMap → Parallel independent requests.
  • concatMap → Sequential updates where order matters.
  • exhaustMap → Prevent duplicate actions like login or payment.

Interview Answer

"All four operators flatten higher-order Observables but differ in how they manage concurrent inner Observables. switchMap cancels previous requests and keeps only the latest, making it ideal for search. mergeMap executes all requests in parallel, concatMap queues them sequentially, and exhaustMap ignores new requests until the current one completes. Choosing the correct operator depends on whether you need cancellation, concurrency, ordering, or duplicate prevention."

question: Subject vs BehaviorSubject vs ReplaySubject vs AsyncSubject.
answer:
Subject is both an Observable and an Observer. It multicasts values to multiple subscribers, but subscribers only receive values emitted after they subscribe.

BehaviorSubject stores the latest value, requires an initial value, and immediately emits the current value to new subscribers.

ReplaySubject stores and replays the last N emitted values to new subscribers.

AsyncSubject emits only the last value, and only after the Observable completes.
tip: Interview Tip: BehaviorSubject is the most commonly used Subject in Angular for shared application state such as the current user, theme, or authentication status.
deep:
What is a Subject?

A Subject is a special type of Observable that can both emit values and be subscribed to. Unlike normal Observables where each subscriber gets its own execution, a Subject shares (multicasts) the same values with all subscribers.

Producer
      ↓
   Subject
   ↙   ↓   ↘
Sub1 Sub2 Sub3

Every subscriber receives the same emitted value.

1. Subject

A Subject does not store previous values. New subscribers only receive values emitted after they subscribe.

const subject = new Subject();

subject.next(1);

subject.subscribe(v => console.log('A:', v));

subject.next(2);
subject.next(3);

// Output
// A: 2
// A: 3

The value 1 is lost because no subscriber existed when it was emitted.

2. BehaviorSubject

BehaviorSubject always stores the latest emitted value. Every new subscriber immediately receives that current value.

const user$ = new BehaviorSubject('Guest');

user$.subscribe(v => console.log('A:', v));
// Guest

user$.next('John');

user$.subscribe(v => console.log('B:', v));
// B immediately receives 'John'

BehaviorSubject requires an initial value.

Real-world Example

export class AuthService {
  currentUser$ = new BehaviorSubject(null);
}

this.authService.currentUser$.subscribe(user => {
  console.log(user);
});

Every component immediately knows the currently logged-in user.

3. ReplaySubject

ReplaySubject stores multiple previous values and replays them to new subscribers.

const replay = new ReplaySubject(2);

replay.next(1);
replay.next(2);
replay.next(3);

replay.subscribe(console.log);

// Output
// 2
// 3

Since the buffer size is 2, only the last two values are replayed.

Real-world Example

  • Chat history.
  • Application logs.
  • Notification history.
  • Recently viewed items.

4. AsyncSubject

AsyncSubject stores only the last value and emits it only after complete() is called.

const subject = new AsyncSubject();

subject.subscribe(console.log);

subject.next(1);
subject.next(2);
subject.next(3);

subject.complete();

// Output
// 3

If complete() is never called, subscribers receive nothing.

Comparison

TypeStores Values?New Subscriber GetsNeeds Initial Value?
Subject❌ NoFuture values only❌ No
BehaviorSubject✅ LatestLatest + future values✅ Yes
ReplaySubject✅ Last NBuffered values + future values❌ No
AsyncSubject✅ LastLast value after complete()❌ No

When to Use Each?

  • Subject → Event bus, button clicks, notifications.
  • BehaviorSubject → Authentication state, current user, theme, shopping cart.
  • ReplaySubject → Chat history, cached responses, activity logs.
  • AsyncSubject → One-time async result after completion.

Angular Example

BehaviorSubject is widely used for shared state between unrelated components.

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class ThemeService {
  theme$ = new BehaviorSubject<'light' | 'dark'>('light');

  changeTheme(theme: 'light' | 'dark') {
    this.theme$.next(theme);
  }
}

Every subscribed component immediately receives the current theme without waiting for another emission.

Interview Tip

The most common interview question is 'Why BehaviorSubject instead of Subject?' Answer: because BehaviorSubject stores the latest value and immediately sends it to new subscribers. This makes it ideal for shared application state such as the logged-in user or application theme.

Interview Answer

"Subject multicasts values but does not retain previous emissions. BehaviorSubject stores the latest value and immediately emits it to new subscribers, making it ideal for shared state. ReplaySubject stores the last N values and replays them to new subscribers, while AsyncSubject emits only the final value after completion. In Angular, BehaviorSubject is the most commonly used because components often need access to the current application state as soon as they subscribe."

question: How do you avoid memory leaks / unsubscribe?
answer:
• async pipe in the template — subscribes and unsubscribes automatically (preferred).
  • takeUntil(destroy$) with a Subject completed in ngOnDestroy.
  • takeUntilDestroyed() in modern Angular.
  • Store the Subscription and call unsubscribe() in ngOnDestroy.

HTTP calls complete on their own, but long-lived streams (Subjects, valueChanges, intervals) leak if you don't clean up.
tip: async pipe first; takeUntil when you must subscribe in the class.
deep:
Why a subscription leaks

Subscribing registers a callback with the source. That callback closes over your component, so the source now holds a reference to it. When Angular destroys the component, the source is still alive and still holding on — so the component is never garbage-collected. Navigate in and out 50 times and you have 50 live components, all still reacting to events.

Which streams actually leak

StreamCompletes by itself?Cleanup needed?
http.get()yes, after 1 emissionno (but harmless)
of() / from()yesno
interval() / timer(0, n)neveryes
fromEvent()neveryes
A Subject you ownonly if you complete ityes
form.valueChangesneveryes
router.events, store.select()neveryes

“HTTP doesn't need unsubscribing” is true but often misused — the leak is almost always a long-lived stream.

The four options, best first

// 1. async pipe — no subscription in your class at all
<div *ngIf="user$ | async as user">{{ user.name }}</div>

// 2. takeUntilDestroyed (Angular 16+) — no boilerplate
data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe();

// 3. takeUntil + destroy$ Subject (the classic)
private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();
ngOnInit()    { this.data$.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$)).subscribe(); }
ngOnDestroy() { this.destroy$.next(); this.destroy$.complete(); }

// 4. manual — fine for one, ugly for many
private sub = new Subscription();
ngOnInit()    { this.sub.add(this.data$.subscribe()); }
ngOnDestroy() { this.sub.unsubscribe(); }   // unsubscribes all children

The bug interviewers look for: takeUntil must be LAST

// BROKEN — switchMap creates a NEW inner subscription
// downstream of takeUntil, so the inner stream never gets cut off
source$.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$), switchMap(() => interval(1000)))

// CORRECT — takeUntil last, so it tears down everything above it
source$.pipe(switchMap(() => interval(1000)), takeUntil(this.destroy$))

Any operator placed after takeUntil can resubscribe to something that outlives the component. This is the single most-cited RxJS leak, and naming it is a strong signal.

Don't forget destroy$.complete()

next() alone fires the notification but leaves the Subject itself subscribed to by takeUntil. complete() releases it.

How to prove it in an interview

Chrome DevTools → Memory → take a heap snapshot, navigate away and back a few times, snapshot again, and filter by your component class name. If the instance count climbs, you're leaking.

How to say it in an interview

“The subscription makes the source hold a reference to my component, so it never gets collected. I default to the async pipe so there's no subscription to manage, and takeUntilDestroyed when I must subscribe in the class. The detail that matters is that takeUntil has to be the last operator — if a switchMap sits after it, the inner stream escapes the teardown and still leaks.”

question: Hot vs cold observables.
answer:
Cold — each subscriber triggers its own producer, getting an independent execution (e.g. an HTTP call runs per subscribe).

Hot — the producer is shared; subscribers see values as they happen (e.g. DOM events, a Subject). Use share() / shareReplay() to make a cold stream hot.
tip: Wrap an HTTP call with shareReplay(1) to avoid firing it once per subscriber.
deep:
The actual definition

It's about where the producer lives.

  • Cold — the producer is created inside the observable, once per subscriber. Everyone gets their own private execution from the start. Unicast.
  • Hot — the producer lives outside and is shared. Subscribers tune in mid-flight and only see what's emitted from then on. Multicast.

Cold is a DVD — everyone watches from the beginning, whenever they press play. Hot is a live broadcast — tune in late, miss the first half.

Which is which

ColdHot
http.get()fromEvent(document,'click')
of(), from()Subject / BehaviorSubject
interval(), timer()anything through share()
most operators' outputstore.select()

Why it bites in practice

const user$ = this.http.get('/me');   // COLD

user$.subscribe();   // request #1
user$.subscribe();   // request #2 — a SECOND network call!

Two async pipes on the same observable = two HTTP calls. Nothing is cached, because each subscribe re-runs the producer from scratch.

Making a cold stream hot

// share() — multicast while at least one subscriber is active
const clicks$ = interval(1000).pipe(share());

// shareReplay(1) — multicast AND replay the last value to latecomers
readonly config$ = this.http.get('/config').pipe(shareReplay(1));

share() gives a late subscriber nothing until the next emission. shareReplay(1) immediately replays the last value — which is what makes it a cache.

The nuance most candidates miss

“Hot vs cold” isn't a property you can inspect — it's about the subscription behaviour. And a Subject is hot, but BehaviorSubject blurs the line: it's hot (shared producer) yet replays its current value to new subscribers, so it feels partly cold. The precise framing is unicast vs multicast plus replay or not.

How to say it in an interview

“Cold means the producer is created per subscription, so every subscriber gets an independent run — that's why two async pipes on one http.get fire two requests. Hot means one shared producer, so subscribers see values from whenever they joined. I make things hot with share(), or shareReplay(1) when late subscribers also need the last value — that's the standard config/cache pattern.”

question: Name common RxJS operators and what they do.
answer: RxJS operators are functions used inside pipe() to transform, filter, combine, or control Observables. Common operators include map, filter, tap, debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged, switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, take, takeUntil, catchError, and forkJoin. They are the building blocks of reactive programming in Angular.
tip: Interview Tip: Know switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap. They are among the most frequently asked RxJS interview topics.
deep:
What are RxJS Operators?

Operators are pure functions that work on Observables. They allow us to transform, filter, combine, delay, or handle asynchronous streams without modifying the original Observable.

Observable
      ↓
pipe()
      ↓
Operators
      ↓
Subscriber

Operators are chained together using the pipe() method.

Transformation Operators

OperatorPurpose
map()Transform emitted values.
switchMap()Cancel previous request and switch to the latest.
mergeMap()Run multiple inner Observables concurrently.
concatMap()Execute inner Observables sequentially.
exhaustMap()Ignore new emissions while one is already running.
of(1,2,3).pipe(
  map(x => x * 10)
);
// 10,20,30

Filtering Operators

OperatorPurpose
filter()Keep matching values.
take()Take first N emissions.
takeUntil()Automatically unsubscribe.
distinctUntilChanged()Ignore duplicate consecutive values.
of(1,2,3,4,5).pipe(
  filter(x => x % 2 === 0)
);
// 2,4

Utility Operators

OperatorPurpose
tap()Perform side effects such as logging.
finalize()Execute cleanup logic when Observable completes.
delay()Delay emissions.
this.http.get('/users').pipe(
  tap(() => console.log('API Called'))
);

Note: Never modify data inside tap(). It is intended only for side effects.

Time-based Operators

OperatorPurpose
debounceTime()Wait until events stop.
throttleTime()Limit execution frequency.
auditTime()Emit the latest value after a time interval.
search.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged()
);

This is commonly used for search boxes to avoid unnecessary API requests.

Error Handling

this.http.get('/users').pipe(
  catchError(err => {
    console.error(err);
    return of([]);
  })
);

catchError() intercepts errors and allows the Observable to recover by returning another Observable.

Combination Operators

OperatorPurpose
combineLatest()Latest values from multiple Observables.
forkJoin()Wait until all Observables complete.
zip()Combine values one by one.
merge()Merge multiple streams.
forkJoin({
  users: this.userService.getUsers(),
  orders: this.orderService.getOrders()
}).subscribe(result => {
  console.log(result.users);
  console.log(result.orders);
});

Real-world Angular Example

this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(search =>
    this.userService.search(search)
  ),
  catchError(() => of([])),
  takeUntil(this.destroy$)
).subscribe(users => {
  this.users = users;
});

This example demonstrates several operators working together:

  • debounceTime() – Wait for the user to stop typing.
  • distinctUntilChanged() – Ignore duplicate searches.
  • switchMap() – Cancel previous API calls.
  • catchError() – Handle API failures.
  • takeUntil() – Prevent memory leaks.

Interview Tip

For Angular interviews, memorize these operators:

  • map()
  • filter()
  • tap()
  • switchMap() ⭐
  • mergeMap()
  • concatMap()
  • forkJoin()
  • combineLatest()
  • debounceTime()
  • distinctUntilChanged()
  • takeUntil()
  • catchError()

Interviewers almost always ask follow-up questions comparing switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap.

Interview Answer

"RxJS operators are functions used inside pipe() to transform, filter, combine, or control Observable streams. Common operators include map, filter, tap, debounceTime, switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, takeUntil, catchError, forkJoin, and combineLatest. In Angular, they are widely used with HttpClient, forms, routing, and state management to build reactive applications."

question: How do you handle errors in RxJS?
answer:
Use catchError to intercept an error and return a fallback observable (or rethrow). retry(n) / retryWhen re-subscribe on failure.

Remember: an error terminates the stream — nothing emits after it, so handle it inside the inner observable if you want the outer stream to survive.
tip: In a switchMap search, put catchError inside the inner call so one failed search doesn't kill the whole stream.
deep:
The rule everything follows: an error is terminal

An observable emits either many nexts then one complete, or one error — and then it's dead. Not paused. Dead. The subscription is torn down and nothing emits again. Every RxJS error question comes back to that.

catchError returns a REPLACEMENT stream

source$.pipe(
  catchError(err => of([]))          // swallow -> emit [] then complete
  // catchError(err => EMPTY)        // swallow -> complete, emit nothing
  // catchError(err => throwError(() => err))  // rethrow after logging
)
ReturnEffect
of(fallback)emits the fallback, then completes
EMPTYcompletes silently, no emission
throwError(...)rethrows to the next handler

The placement bug — this is the whole question

// BROKEN — one failed search kills the search box forever
input$.pipe(
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q)),
  catchError(() => of([]))     // outer: catches, then the OUTER stream completes
)

// CORRECT — the error dies inside the inner stream
input$.pipe(
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q).pipe(
    catchError(() => of([]))   // inner: outer never sees an error
  ))
)

Outer catchError = the stream dies after the first failure. The user types again and nothing happens — a bug that looks like a mystery in production and is trivially explained by this rule. Same applies to NgRx effects: an uncaught error kills the effect for the app's lifetime.

Retrying

req$.pipe(
  retry({ count: 3, delay: (_e, i) => timer(2 ** i * 1000) }),  // backoff
  catchError(err => { this.toast.error('Failed'); return EMPTY; })
)

retry works by re-subscribing to the source — which only makes sense because the source is cold. Never blanket-retry: a 400 or 401 will fail identically three times. Retry transient failures (network, 503) only.

Where errors belong architecturally

  • HTTP interceptor — cross-cutting: 401 → refresh token, 5xx → toast. Keeps components clean.
  • Global ErrorHandler — last resort, log to Sentry.
  • Inner catchError — when this stream must survive.

How to say it in an interview

“An error is terminal, so the question is always where you catch it. catchError returns a replacement observable — of([]) to fall back, EMPTY to swallow, throwError to rethrow. The classic bug is catching on the outer stream of a switchMap search: it handles the first error and then the whole search box is dead. Put it on the inner observable so the outer never sees it. Cross-cutting stuff like 401s I handle once in an interceptor.”

question: combineLatest vs forkJoin vs merge vs zip.
answer: combineLatest emits the latest value from each Observable whenever any source emits (after all have emitted at least once). forkJoin waits for all Observables to complete and emits their last values once (similar to Promise.all()). merge emits values from all Observables as they arrive without waiting. zip combines values by their emission order (index), waiting until each Observable has a corresponding value.
tip: Remember: combineLatest = Latest Values, forkJoin = Wait for All, merge = As They Arrive, zip = Pair by Index.
deep:
Why do we need Combination Operators?

Real-world Angular applications often work with multiple asynchronous streams such as HTTP requests, form controls, route parameters, timers, and WebSockets. RxJS provides combination operators to coordinate these streams.

1. combineLatest()

combineLatest() waits until every Observable has emitted at least once. After that, whenever any Observable emits, it combines the latest value from every source.

const a$ = interval(1000);
const b$ = interval(2000);

combineLatest([a$, b$])
  .subscribe(console.log);
Time →
A: 0---1---2---3---4
B: ----0-------1----

Output:
[1,0]
[2,0]
[3,1]
[4,1]

Use Cases:

  • Search + Filters
  • Form validation
  • Dashboard widgets
  • Route params + Query params

2. forkJoin()

forkJoin() waits until every Observable completes, then emits one object or array containing the final value from each Observable.

forkJoin({
  users: this.http.get('/users'),
  orders: this.http.get('/orders')
}).subscribe(result => {
  console.log(result.users);
  console.log(result.orders);
});

Use Cases:

  • Load dashboard data.
  • Fetch multiple APIs on page load.
  • Parallel HTTP requests.

Important: If one Observable never completes (for example interval()), forkJoin() never emits.

3. merge()

merge() combines multiple Observables into one by emitting values immediately as they arrive. It does not preserve order.

const a$ = interval(1000);
const b$ = interval(1500);

merge(a$, b$)
  .subscribe(console.log);
Time →
A: 0---1---2---3
B: --0----1----2

Output:
0
0
1
2
1
3
2

Use Cases:

  • Merge multiple event streams.
  • Combine button clicks.
  • Listen to multiple WebSocket streams.

4. zip()

zip() waits until every Observable has produced the next value, then emits them together as a pair.

const a$ = of('A', 'B', 'C');
const b$ = of(1, 2, 3);

zip(a$, b$)
  .subscribe(console.log);
Output:
['A',1]
['B',2]
['C',3]

Use Cases:

  • Pair related data.
  • Synchronize streams.
  • Process records one by one.

Comparison

OperatorWaits?EmitsTypical Use
combineLatestUntil each emits onceLatest values whenever any changesReactive UI, Forms
forkJoinUntil all completeOne final combined valueParallel API Calls
mergeNoImmediately as values arriveEvents, WebSockets
zipYes (per emission)Pairs values by indexSynchronizing streams

Real-world Angular Examples

combineLatest() - Update search results whenever either the search text or selected category changes.

combineLatest([
  this.search.valueChanges,
  this.category.valueChanges
]).subscribe(([text, category]) => {
  this.searchProducts(text, category);
});

forkJoin() - Load multiple APIs when opening a dashboard.

forkJoin({
  users: this.userService.getUsers(),
  products: this.productService.getProducts(),
  orders: this.orderService.getOrders()
}).subscribe();

merge() - Listen to multiple button clicks.

merge(saveClick$, cancelClick$)
  .subscribe(console.log);

zip() - Pair usernames with generated IDs.

zip(usernames$, ids$)
  .subscribe(console.log);

Interview Tip

The most common interview question is 'When would you use combineLatest instead of forkJoin?' Answer: combineLatest is for continuously changing streams like forms and filters, while forkJoin is for one-time parallel HTTP requests where you need all results before continuing.

Interview Answer

"combineLatest emits whenever any source changes after each has emitted once, making it ideal for reactive UI updates. forkJoin waits for all Observables to complete and emits once, making it perfect for parallel API calls. merge emits values immediately as they arrive from multiple streams, while zip pairs emissions by index, waiting until every Observable has produced the next value. In Angular, combineLatest is commonly used with forms, forkJoin with HttpClient, merge with event streams, and zip for synchronized data processing."

question: of vs from vs fromEvent.
answer:
• of(1,2,3) — emits the given values then completes.
  • from(arrayOrPromise) — turns an array/iterable/Promise into an observable.
  • fromEvent(el,'click') — an observable of DOM events.
tip: from unpacks an array element-by-element; of emits the array as a single value.
code:
of([1, 2, 3]);   // one emission: [1,2,3]
from([1, 2, 3]); // three emissions: 1, 2, 3

question: What is the pipe() method?
answer: pipe() is a method on an Observable that lets you chain multiple RxJS operators together. Each operator receives the output of the previous one and returns a new Observable without modifying the original stream. This makes data transformation, filtering, error handling, and asynchronous operations easy to compose in a readable way.
tip: Think of pipe() as an assembly line. Each operator performs one job and passes the result to the next operator.
deep:
Why do we need pipe()?

Observables emit values over time. Before those values reach the subscriber, we often need to transform them, filter them, log them, delay them, or handle errors. Instead of writing all this logic inside subscribe(), RxJS provides pipe() to process the data step by step.

Observable
      ↓
pipe()
      ↓
Operator 1
      ↓
Operator 2
      ↓
Operator 3
      ↓
Subscriber

Each operator receives the output of the previous operator.

Simple Example

of(1,2,3,4).pipe(
  map(x => x * 10),
  filter(x => x > 20)
).subscribe(console.log);

// Output
30
40

Execution happens like this:

1,2,3,4
      ↓
map(x * 10)
10,20,30,40
      ↓
filter(x > 20)
30,40
      ↓
subscribe()

pipe() does NOT modify the original Observable

Every operator returns a new Observable.

const source$ = of(1,2,3);

const doubled$ = source$.pipe(
  map(x => x * 2)
);

source$.subscribe(console.log);
// 1 2 3

doubled$.subscribe(console.log);
// 2 4 6

The original Observable remains unchanged.

Common Operators inside pipe()

OperatorPurpose
map()Transform values.
filter()Keep matching values.
tap()Logging or debugging.
debounceTime()Wait before emitting.
switchMap()Switch to another Observable.
catchError()Handle errors.
takeUntil()Auto unsubscribe.

Real-world Angular Example

this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(text =>
    this.userService.search(text)
  ),
  catchError(() => of([]))
).subscribe(users => {
  this.users = users;
});

Here every operator has one responsibility:

  1. debounceTime() → Wait until the user stops typing.
  2. distinctUntilChanged() → Ignore duplicate searches.
  3. switchMap() → Cancel the previous API call.
  4. catchError() → Handle API failures.
  5. subscribe() → Receive the final result.

Why not write everything inside subscribe()?

// ❌ Not Recommended
this.http.get('/users').subscribe(users => {
  const active = users.filter(u => u.active);
  console.log(active);
});

Instead:

// ✅ Recommended
this.http.get('/users').pipe(
  map(users => users.filter(u => u.active))
).subscribe(console.log);

The data is transformed before it reaches the subscriber, making the code cleaner and easier to test.

Execution Order

Operators execute from left to right.

source$
  .pipe(
    map(...),
    filter(...),
    tap(...),
    catchError(...)
  )
  .subscribe();

The output of map() becomes the input to filter(), then to tap(), and so on.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask 'Why do we use pipe()?' A good answer is: pipe() allows us to compose multiple RxJS operators into a readable processing pipeline. Each operator performs one transformation and returns a new Observable, making the code modular, reusable, and easier to maintain.

Interview Answer

"pipe() is used to chain RxJS operators together. Each operator receives the output of the previous operator and returns a new Observable. This lets us transform, filter, combine, delay, or handle errors in a clean, declarative way before the data reaches subscribe(). In Angular, pipe() is commonly used with HttpClient, forms, routing, and state management."

question: Why pair debounceTime with distinctUntilChanged in search?
answer: debounceTime waits for the user to pause typing; distinctUntilChanged drops emissions equal to the previous one (e.g. type then delete back to the same term) so you don't fire a duplicate request.
tip: This trio — debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged, switchMap — is the canonical search pattern.

question: What does startWith do?
answer: startWith() emits one or more values immediately when an Observable is subscribed to, before the source Observable starts emitting its own values. It is commonly used to provide an initial value so the UI has something to display while waiting for real data.
tip: Think of startWith() as adding a default value at the beginning of an Observable stream.
deep:
What does startWith() do?

startWith() prepends one or more values to an Observable. These values are emitted immediately when someone subscribes, before the original Observable produces its own values.

Source Observable
      ↓
startWith('Initial')
      ↓
Subscriber

Output:
Initial
Actual Values...

Simple Example

of(2, 3, 4).pipe(
  startWith(1)
).subscribe(console.log);

// Output
1
2
3
4

The value 1 is emitted before the source Observable starts.

Why is it Useful?

Many Observables do not emit immediately. For example, valueChanges only emits after the user changes a value.

this.searchControl.valueChanges
  .subscribe(console.log);

Initially, nothing is emitted.

Using startWith():

this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  startWith('')
).subscribe(console.log);

The subscriber immediately receives an empty string, allowing the UI to initialize.

Real-world Angular Example 1 - Search

this.filteredUsers$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  startWith(''),
  switchMap(text =>
    this.userService.search(text)
  )
);

Without startWith(''), the first API call would happen only after the user typed something. With it, the full user list loads immediately.

Real-world Angular Example 2 - Initial Empty List

this.items$ = this.http.get('/api/items').pipe(
  startWith([])
);

The template receives an empty array immediately, preventing undefined errors while waiting for the HTTP response.

Real-world Angular Example 3 - Loading State

this.loading$ = this.http.get('/users').pipe(
  map(() => false),
  startWith(true)
);

The UI displays a loading spinner immediately, then changes to false when the request finishes.

Comparison

Without startWith()With startWith()
No initial valueInitial value emitted immediately
UI waits for first emissionUI can render immediately
Possible undefined stateDefault state available

Common Uses

  • Initialize form controls.
  • Display an empty list before API data arrives.
  • Show loading state.
  • Trigger an initial search.
  • Provide default filter values.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask why startWith('') is used with valueChanges. The answer is that valueChanges does not emit until the user changes the input. startWith() provides an initial value so filtering or searching can happen immediately.

Interview Answer

"startWith() emits one or more initial values before the source Observable begins emitting. It is commonly used in Angular to initialize UI state, provide default values, trigger an initial search, or avoid undefined values while waiting for asynchronous data."

question: Show the canonical type-ahead search pipeline.
answer: Combine the four operators every interviewer wants: debounceTime (wait for a pause), distinctUntilChanged (skip duplicate terms), switchMap (cancel stale requests), and catchError inside the inner call (so one failure doesn't kill the stream).
tip: Put catchError on the inner observable, not the outer — otherwise the stream dies after one error.
code:
input$.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q).pipe(catchError(() => of([]))))
)
deep:
The full pipeline

results$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),                    // 1. wait for a pause in typing
  distinctUntilChanged(),               // 2. skip if the term didn't change
  filter(term => term.length >= 2),     // 3. don't search on 1 char
  switchMap(term => this.api.search(term).pipe(   // 4. cancel stale requests
    catchError(() => of([]))            // 5. inner — keep the stream alive
  )),
  takeUntilDestroyed()                  // 6. clean up
);

In the template: results$ | async — no manual subscription at all.

Why each operator earns its place

OperatorRemove it and…
debounceTime(300)a request per keystroke — "angular" = 7 calls
distinctUntilChanged()type "a", delete, retype "a" → duplicate call
filter(len >= 2)"a" returns half the database
switchMaprace condition — see below
inner catchErrorone failed search kills the box permanently

The race condition — why switchMap, not mergeMap

You type "ang" then "angular". Two requests are in flight. The "ang" response is slower and lands second.

  • mergeMap — both complete; the stale "ang" results overwrite "angular". The user sees results for a query they've already replaced. Intermittent, timing-dependent, miserable to debug.
  • switchMap — subscribing to the new inner observable unsubscribes the previous one, which cancels the in-flight XHR. Only the latest response can arrive.

That cancellation is real: Angular's HttpClient calls xhr.abort() on unsubscribe, so you'll see the request go red/cancelled in the Network tab. That's the answer to "prove switchMap cancels".

Adding a loading indicator

switchMap(term => this.api.search(term).pipe(
  catchError(() => of([])),
  finalize(() => this.loading.set(false))   // always runs
))

Where else the same shape appears

Autocomplete, filter-as-you-type tables, live validation (username availability), and NgRx effects handling a search action. Same five operators.

How to say it in an interview

“debounceTime to wait for a pause, distinctUntilChanged to drop repeats, a filter for minimum length, then switchMap into the API with catchError inside. switchMap is the important choice — it cancels the in-flight request, so a slow earlier response can't overwrite the latest results. With mergeMap you'd get an intermittent race where stale results win.”

question: How do you poll an API every N seconds with RxJS?
answer: Use timer(0, N) to emit immediately then every N ms, and switchMap to the request (so a slow response doesn't overlap the next tick). Stop with takeUntil.
tip: switchMap prevents overlapping requests if one poll is slower than the interval.

question: How do you retry a failed request with exponential backoff?
answer: Use retry with a delay function (RxJS 7.4+) that returns an increasing delay per attempt, or retryWhen in older versions. Cap the number of retries so it doesn't loop forever.
tip: Exponential backoff avoids hammering a struggling server — mention capping the retries.
deep:
The modern form (RxJS 7.4+)

req$.pipe(
  retry({
    count: 3,
    delay: (error, retryCount) => {
      if (error.status >= 400 && error.status < 500) throw error;  // don't retry 4xx
      return timer(2 ** retryCount * 1000);   // 2s, 4s, 8s
    }
  }),
  catchError(err => { this.toast.error('Service unavailable'); return EMPTY; })
)

retryWhen is deprecated — knowing that is a quick credibility marker. Its replacement is the delay callback above, which is far easier to read.

How retry actually works

It re-subscribes to the source. That's only meaningful because HTTP observables are cold — re-subscribing genuinely re-issues the request. Retrying a hot observable would just re-listen, not re-run anything. It's a neat place to show the cold/hot concept paying off.

Why exponential, and why cap it

A struggling server fails, so every client retries at once, so it gets more load and fails harder — a retry storm. Exponential backoff spreads attempts out (2s, 4s, 8s) to give it room to recover. The count cap stops you retrying forever and turning a transient blip into an infinite loop.

What you must NOT retry

RetryNever retry
network / timeout400 — the payload is wrong; it'll fail identically
503, 502, 429401 / 403 — refresh the token instead
idempotent GET/PUT404 — it won't appear
non-idempotent POST — may create duplicates

Blanket retry(3) on every request is the mistake: retrying a POST three times can create three orders. Mentioning idempotency here is exactly the seniority signal.

Jitter — the detail almost nobody gives

delay: (_e, i) => timer(2 ** i * 1000 + Math.random() * 1000)

Pure exponential backoff still synchronises clients — they all retry at exactly 2s. Random jitter de-synchronises them. This is standard practice in AWS/Google client libraries.

In a real app: put it in an interceptor

intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
  return next.handle(req).pipe(
    retry({ count: 2, delay: (e, i) =>
      e.status >= 500 ? timer(2 ** i * 1000) : throwError(() => e) })
  );
}

How to say it in an interview

“retry({ count, delay }) since RxJS 7.4 — retryWhen's deprecated. It works by re-subscribing, which only re-issues the request because HTTP is cold. I use exponential backoff with jitter so clients don't all retry in lockstep and cause a retry storm, cap the count, and — most importantly — only retry transient failures. Retrying a 400 fails identically three times, and retrying a POST can create duplicate records.”

question: How do you cache an HTTP result across subscribers?
answer:
Pipe the request through shareReplay(1): the underlying call runs once, and every current and future subscriber gets the last emitted value — a one-line in-memory cache.

The buffer size 1 means "replay the most recent value". Without shareReplay, each subscriber re-runs the cold HTTP call.
tip: shareReplay(1) turns a per-subscriber HTTP call into a single shared, cached one.
deep:
The pattern

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class ConfigService {
  readonly config$ = this.http.get<Config>('/api/config').pipe(
    shareReplay(1)
  );
}
// 10 components subscribe -> 1 HTTP call, 10 subscribers get the value

Note it's a field, not a method. getConfig() returning a new piped observable each call would rebuild the pipeline and defeat the cache entirely — a subtle and very common mistake.

The trap: shareReplay can leak

// LEAK — the source stays subscribed FOREVER, even with zero subscribers
interval(1000).pipe(shareReplay(1)).subscribe(...);  // timer never stops

// SAFE — drops the source when the last subscriber leaves
interval(1000).pipe(shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }))

By default refCount is false: shareReplay subscribes to the source and never unsubscribes, keeping it alive for the app's lifetime. Harmless for a finite http.get(), a real leak for an infinite source.

So which do you want?

refCount: false (default)refCount: true
Source when subscribers hit 0stays subscribedunsubscribed
Cached valuekept foreverdiscarded
Good forconfig/lookup cachinginfinite sources
Riskleaks infinite sourcesre-fetches after last unsubscribe

Counter-intuitively, for HTTP caching the default (refCount: false) is what you want — it's precisely what keeps the value cached after everyone unsubscribes. Adding refCount: true to an HTTP cache means navigating away and back re-fetches.

share() vs shareReplay()

share() multicasts but replays nothing — a subscriber arriving after the HTTP response gets nothing, forever, because the call already completed. That's why caching always uses shareReplay(1).

Cache invalidation

private refresh$ = new BehaviorSubject<void>(undefined);

readonly users$ = this.refresh$.pipe(
  switchMap(() => this.http.get<User[]>('/users')),
  shareReplay(1)
);
refresh() { this.refresh$.next(); }   // re-fetch, all subscribers update

How to say it in an interview

“shareReplay(1) multicasts the cold HTTP call so it runs once and replays the last value to late subscribers — but it has to be a shared field, not a method, or you rebuild the pipeline each call. The catch is refCount: it defaults to false, so the source is never unsubscribed. For an http.get that's exactly what you want for caching; for an interval it's a leak, so there I pass refCount: true.”

question: What does scan do (vs reduce)?
answer: scan is like reduce but emits the accumulated value on every emission, not just at the end — perfect for accumulating state over a stream (a running total, or building up a list from actions).
tip: scan is the RxJS building block for a reducer-style state stream.
deep:
scan vs reduce

const nums$ = of(1, 2, 3);

nums$.pipe(reduce((a, n) => a + n, 0)).subscribe(console.log);
// 6                      -- ONE value, only on complete

nums$.pipe(scan((a, n) => a + n, 0)).subscribe(console.log);
// 1, 3, 6                -- every intermediate value

Same signature, different timing. reduce waits for complete and emits once; scan emits the running accumulator on every source emission.

The consequence: reduce on an infinite stream (interval, a Subject, clicks) emits nothing, ever — it's waiting for a completion that never comes. On live streams you almost always want scan.

Why it matters: scan is a reducer

scan((state, action) => reducer(state, action), initialState)

That signature is identical to Redux/NgRx. A store is, at its core, a stream of actions run through scan and multicast:

// A working mini-store in 5 lines
private actions$ = new Subject<Action>();

readonly state$ = this.actions$.pipe(
  scan((state, action) => reducer(state, action), initialState),
  shareReplay(1)          // late subscribers get current state
);

dispatch(action: Action) { this.actions$.next(action); }

Being able to write that is a genuinely strong answer to “do you actually need NgRx?” — it shows you understand what the library is doing rather than treating it as magic.

Everyday uses

// accumulate paginated results
loadMore$.pipe(
  concatMap(page => this.api.getPage(page)),
  scan((all, page) => [...all, ...page], [] as Item[])
)

// a running counter
clicks$.pipe(scan(n => n + 1, 0))

// undo history
actions$.pipe(scan((hist, a) => [...hist, a], []))

Keep it pure

// BROKEN — mutating breaks OnPush and replay
scan((acc, x) => { acc.push(x); return acc; }, [])
// CORRECT — new reference each time
scan((acc, x) => [...acc, x], [])

Mutating the accumulator returns the same reference, so OnPush and distinctUntilChanged see "no change" and skip the update.

How to say it in an interview

“Same reducer signature, different timing: reduce emits once on complete, scan emits the accumulator every time. So on an infinite stream reduce never emits at all. scan is really a reducer over a stream — scan plus shareReplay(1) over a Subject of actions is essentially a miniature NgRx store. And the accumulator has to stay immutable or OnPush won't see the change.”

question: combineLatest vs withLatestFrom.
answer: combineLatest emits when any source emits. withLatestFrom emits only when the source emits, pulling in the latest value of the others — use it when one stream is the trigger and the rest are just context.
tip: withLatestFrom = 'when A fires, also give me the current B' — one driver, others passive.
deep:
Trigger vs context

The distinction is who is allowed to cause an emission.

  • combineLatest([a$, b$]) — either can fire it. Emits whenever any source emits (once all have a value).
  • a$.pipe(withLatestFrom(b$)) — only a$ fires it. b$ is passive context, sampled at that moment.
a$: --1-----2-----3---
b$: ----A-----B-------

combineLatest:  ----1A--2A-2B--3B---   // every change from either
withLatestFrom: --------2A----3B---   // only when a$ emits (1 dropped: no b yet)

The classic use: save button + form value

// The click is the trigger; the form value is just context.
this.saveClicks$.pipe(
  withLatestFrom(this.form.valueChanges),
  switchMap(([_click, form]) => this.api.save(form))
).subscribe();

With combineLatest this would be a bug: every keystroke changes valueChanges, which would fire a save on every character typed. The user never clicked. That's the answer to "why not combineLatest here?"

The gotcha: it silently drops early emissions

trigger$.pipe(withLatestFrom(config$))   // config$ hasn't emitted yet

If the "other" observable has no value yet, the trigger's emission is dropped silently — no error, nothing. Click save before config loads and nothing happens. Fix by making the other stream a BehaviorSubject/shareReplay(1) so it always has a current value.

Argument order

a$.pipe(withLatestFrom(b$, c$))
  .subscribe(([a, b, c]) => ...);   // source is ALWAYS first

Where you'll actually use it — NgRx effects

saveOrder$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(saveOrder),
  withLatestFrom(this.store.select(selectUserId)),   // action triggers; state is context
  switchMap(([action, userId]) => this.api.save(action.order, userId))
));

Using combineLatest there would re-run the effect whenever the store changed — firing saves nobody asked for. (Modern NgRx prefers concatLatestFrom, which is the lazy version — the selector isn't subscribed until the action arrives.)

How to say it in an interview

“combineLatest emits when any source emits; withLatestFrom only when the source emits and just samples the others. So it's for one trigger plus passive context — a save click pulling in the current form value. With combineLatest there, every keystroke would trigger a save. The gotcha is that if the other stream hasn't emitted yet the trigger is dropped silently, so I make sure it's a BehaviorSubject or shareReplay'd.”

question: What is finalize used for?
answer: finalize runs a callback when the observable completes, errors, or is unsubscribed — the RxJS equivalent of finally. Common use: hide a loading spinner regardless of success or failure.
tip: finalize is the clean place to turn off a loading flag — it always runs.

question: debounceTime vs throttleTime vs auditTime vs sampleTime.
answer:
• debounceTime — emit after a quiet gap (last value).
  • throttleTime — emit the first value, then ignore for a window.
  • auditTime — ignore for a window, then emit the latest value.
  • sampleTime — emit the latest value on a fixed interval.
tip: debounce for search, throttle for scroll/resize, sample for periodic snapshots.
deep:
The four, on one timeline

Source emits rapidly: 1-2-3-------4-5---

OperatorEmitsWhich valueTimer starts on
debounceTime(t)after t of silencethe lastevery emission (resets)
throttleTime(t)immediately, then mutes for tthe firstfirst emission
auditTime(t)at the end of the windowthe latestfirst emission
sampleTime(t)on a fixed clockthe latestindependent interval

The two axes that actually distinguish them

  1. First value or last value? throttleTime is the only one that's eager — it emits immediately, then goes quiet. The other three emit a value from the end of some window.
  2. Does the timer reset? Only debounceTime restarts on every emission — which is why a user typing continuously for 10 seconds produces zero emissions. That's the debounce gotcha: with a fast typist and debounceTime(1000), the search never fires until they pause.

Choosing in practice

  • Search box → debounceTime(300). You only care about the final term, and waiting for a pause is exactly right.
  • Scroll / resize handler → throttleTime(100). You want an immediate response (debounce would feel laggy — nothing happens until scrolling stops), just not 60×/second.
  • Button double-click guard → throttleTime(1000) — first click wins, rest ignored. (Though exhaustMap is better if the guard is about an in-flight request.)
  • mousemove → render → auditTime(16). Throttle would give you the stale first position of each window; audit gives the latest. For following a cursor, latest is what you want.
  • Live price/telemetry snapshot → sampleTime(1000). "Whatever the value is each second."

throttleTime's config (the follow-up)

throttleTime(100, asyncScheduler, { leading: true, trailing: false })  // default
throttleTime(100, asyncScheduler, { leading: false, trailing: true })  // ~= auditTime

Default is leading-only. Set trailing: true and you also get the last value of each window — which is how throttleTime and auditTime converge.

The trap: debounce ≠ throttle for scroll

Using debounceTime on scroll means your sticky header does nothing while the user scrolls and snaps into place after they stop. Feels broken. Scroll wants throttleTime or auditTime.

How to say it in an interview

“debounceTime waits for a pause and emits the last value — search. throttleTime emits the first immediately then mutes — scroll, where you need an instant response. auditTime waits out the window then emits the latest — better than throttle for mousemove, since throttle gives you a stale position. sampleTime is on its own clock. The debounce gotcha is that the timer resets, so someone typing continuously never triggers a search at all.”

question: What are higher-order observables and *All operators?
answer: A higher-order observable emits other observables. Flattening operators subscribe to the inner ones: mergeAll (concurrent), concatAll (in order), switchAll (cancel previous). The *Map variants (mergeMap, concatMap…) are just map + *All.
tip: switchMap === map(...) + switchAll — knowing this shows deep RxJS understanding.
code:
source$.pipe(map(id => this.load(id)), concatAll());
// same as: source$.pipe(concatMap(id => this.load(id)))
deep:
What makes an observable "higher-order"

Ordinary map returning an observable gives you a stream of streams:

ids$.pipe(map(id => this.http.get(`/user/${id}`)))
// Type: Observable<Observable<User>>  -- nested!
// Subscribing gives you Observable objects, NOT users. And no HTTP fires,
// because nobody subscribed to the inner ones.

A flattening operator subscribes to each inner observable and re-emits its values on the outer stream, collapsing Observable<Observable<T>> → Observable<T>.

The identity that makes it click

source$.pipe(map(fn), mergeAll())   ===   source$.pipe(mergeMap(fn))
source$.pipe(map(fn), concatAll())  ===   source$.pipe(concatMap(fn))
source$.pipe(map(fn), switchAll())  ===   source$.pipe(switchMap(fn))
source$.pipe(map(fn), exhaustAll()) ===   source$.pipe(exhaustMap(fn))

Every *Map operator is literally map + the matching *All. Saying this out loud is a strong senior signal — it reframes four "different" operators as one idea (map) plus a concurrency strategy.

The four strategies

OperatorWhen a new inner arrives while one is activeUse for
mergeAllrun both concurrentlyindependent parallel work
concatAllqueue it, preserve orderordered writes
switchAllcancel the previoussearch / latest-wins
exhaustAllignore the new onedouble-click submit guard

Limiting concurrency

// mergeAll/mergeMap take a concurrency limit — an underrated answer
urls$.pipe(mergeMap(url => this.http.get(url), 3));  // at most 3 in flight
// concatMap === mergeMap with concurrency 1

“How would you fetch 500 URLs without hammering the server?” → mergeMap with a concurrency cap.

Where you meet higher-order observables by accident

// Forgetting to flatten — the bug
this.search$.pipe(map(q => this.api.search(q))).subscribe(x => console.log(x));
// logs: Observable {...} — and no request ever fires

How to say it in an interview

“A higher-order observable emits observables — you get that the moment you map to an HTTP call. The *All operators subscribe to the inner streams and flatten them, and every *Map is exactly map plus the matching *All — switchMap is map + switchAll. So the real choice isn't four operators, it's one concurrency strategy: run in parallel, queue, cancel, or ignore.”

question: What is takeUntilDestroyed?
answer: (Angular 16+) An operator that auto-completes a subscription when the component/directive is destroyed — replacing the manual destroy$ Subject + takeUntil boilerplate. Call it in an injection context, or pass a DestroyRef.
tip: It's the modern, boilerplate-free way to prevent subscription leaks.
deep:
What it replaces

// Before — 5 lines of boilerplate in every component
private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();
ngOnInit() {
  this.data$.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$)).subscribe();
}
ngOnDestroy() {
  this.destroy$.next();
  this.destroy$.complete();
}

// After (Angular 16+)
data$ = inject(DataService).data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed());

It hooks into DestroyRef and completes the stream when the component, directive, pipe, or service is destroyed.

The rule that trips everyone: injection context

// ✔ field initializer — injection context
export class MyComp {
  data$ = this.svc.data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed());
}

// ✔ constructor — injection context
constructor() {
  this.svc.data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe();
}

// ✘ ngOnInit — NOT an injection context -> runtime error
ngOnInit() {
  this.svc.data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe();  // throws NG0203
}

// ✔ ngOnInit — pass the DestroyRef explicitly
private destroyRef = inject(DestroyRef);
ngOnInit() {
  this.svc.data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef)).subscribe();
}

Calling it with no argument outside an injection context throws NG0203. Knowing the DestroyRef escape hatch is the practical half of the answer — most real subscriptions do start in ngOnInit.

Still last in the pipe

// ✘ inner interval escapes the teardown
source$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed(), switchMap(() => interval(1000)))
// ✔
source$.pipe(switchMap(() => interval(1000)), takeUntilDestroyed())

takeUntilDestroyed inherits takeUntil's positioning rule — it must come last, or operators after it can resubscribe to something that outlives the component.

DestroyRef directly

// For non-RxJS cleanup
private destroyRef = inject(DestroyRef);
constructor() {
  const id = setInterval(tick, 1000);
  this.destroyRef.onDestroy(() => clearInterval(id));
}

Useful in services and functional guards/interceptors, which have no ngOnDestroy.

Where it sits in the hierarchy

async pipe → toSignal → takeUntilDestroyed → takeUntil(destroy$). The first two mean no subscription to manage at all; reach for takeUntilDestroyed when you genuinely must subscribe imperatively.

How to say it in an interview

“Angular 16's replacement for the destroy$ Subject boilerplate — it uses DestroyRef to complete the stream when the component is destroyed. The catch is it must run in an injection context, so a bare call in ngOnInit throws NG0203 — there I inject DestroyRef and pass it in. And like takeUntil, it has to be the last operator.”

question: How do you write a custom operator?
answer: A pipeable operator is just a function that takes a source observable and returns a new one — usually by composing existing operators with pipe. This makes reusable, named stream transformations.
tip: A custom operator is often just a named composition of built-in operators.

question: What are RxJS schedulers?
answer: A scheduler controls when and on what context emissions happen — e.g. asyncScheduler (macrotask), asapScheduler (microtask), animationFrameScheduler (rAF). Used for fine timing control and for virtual time in tests (TestScheduler).
tip: Most code never touches schedulers directly — but marble tests rely on the TestScheduler.
deep:
What a scheduler is

A scheduler answers "when does this work run?". It's a combination of a clock and an execution context — every RxJS operator that involves timing delegates to one. Most code never names one explicitly, but they're always there.

The four

SchedulerRuns workAnalogous to
none (default)synchronously, right nowa direct call
queueSchedulersynchronously, but queueda trampoline (avoids stack overflow in recursion)
asapSchedulermicrotaskPromise.resolve().then()
asyncSchedulermacrotasksetInterval
animationFrameSchedulerbefore the next repaintrequestAnimationFrame

delay, interval, timer, debounceTime all default to asyncScheduler.

subscribeOn vs observeOn — the classic pairing

source$.pipe(
  subscribeOn(asyncScheduler),   // WHERE/WHEN the subscription (producer) starts
  observeOn(animationFrameScheduler)  // WHERE/WHEN each emission is DELIVERED
)
  • subscribeOn affects the whole chain's start — it only matters once, and position in the pipe is irrelevant.
  • observeOn affects everything downstream of it — so position matters.

Where it's genuinely useful

// Render-heavy stream — don't do layout work more than once per frame
mouseMove$.pipe(observeOn(animationFrameScheduler)).subscribe(pos => this.draw(pos));

// Escape ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError
value$.pipe(observeOn(asapScheduler)).subscribe(v => this.value = v);

That second one is the practical Angular payoff: deferring to a microtask pushes the update into the next change-detection turn, which is a cleaner fix than setTimeout(0).

The real reason to know them: marble testing

it('debounces', () => {
  const scheduler = new TestScheduler((actual, expected) =>
    expect(actual).toEqual(expected));

  scheduler.run(({ cold, expectObservable }) => {
    const source$ = cold('a-b-c------|');
    const result$ = source$.pipe(debounceTime(30));
    expectObservable(result$).toBe('--------c--|');
  });
});

TestScheduler uses virtual time: a debounceTime(300) test runs in microseconds instead of taking 300ms, and there's no flakiness. This is the answer to "how do you test time-based RxJS without fakeAsync?"

Honest framing

If asked "do you use schedulers?", the credible answer is: rarely by name in app code — but constantly via TestScheduler, and occasionally animationFrameScheduler for render-bound streams. Claiming daily use would be a red flag.

How to say it in an interview

“A scheduler decides when and in what context work runs — sync, microtask, macrotask, or animation frame. subscribeOn controls when the producer starts, observeOn where emissions are delivered downstream. I don't reach for them often in app code, apart from animationFrameScheduler for render-heavy streams — but TestScheduler matters a lot: marble tests run time-based operators in virtual time, so a 300ms debounce test is instant and never flaky.”

question: How do you convert an Observable to a Promise?
answer: Use firstValueFrom(obs$) (resolves with the first emission) or lastValueFrom(obs$) (waits for completion). These replaced the deprecated .toPromise(). Handy when awaiting a one-shot stream in async/await code.
tip: .toPromise() is deprecated — use firstValueFrom / lastValueFrom.

question: Why does my HTTP call fire twice with two async pipes?
answer: Observables are cold — each subscription re-runs the producer, so two async pipes on the same http.get() trigger two requests. Fix by multicasting with shareReplay(1), or subscribe once and reuse the value.
tip: Two async pipes on one HTTP observable = two network calls unless you shareReplay it.
deep:
What's happening

// component
user$ = this.http.get<User>('/me');
<!-- template -->
<span>{{ (user$ | async)?.name }}</span>
<img  [src]="(user$ | async)?.avatar">
<!-- 2 async pipes = 2 subscriptions = 2 GET /me requests -->

HttpClient observables are cold: the request isn't made when you create the observable, it's made on subscribe. Each async pipe subscribes independently, so each fires its own request. Nothing is shared or cached by default.

Three fixes, in order of preference

// 1. Subscribe once in the template with *ngIf ... as
<ng-container *ngIf="user$ | async as user">
  <span>{{ user.name }}</span>
  <img [src]="user.avatar">
</ng-container>
// or Angular 17+:  @if (user$ | async; as user) { ... }

// 2. Multicast the source
user$ = this.http.get<User>('/me').pipe(shareReplay(1));

// 3. Convert to a signal (Angular 16+) — one subscription
user = toSignal(this.http.get<User>('/me'));

Fix 1 is best when the data is used in one template region: one subscription, no extra operator, and it doubles as the loading guard. Fix 2 is right when several components need it — put it in a service.

The related trap: it's not just async pipes

const req$ = this.http.post('/orders', body);
req$.subscribe();   // order created
req$.subscribe();   // ANOTHER order created!

Because cold means "re-run the producer per subscribe", subscribing twice to a POST creates two records. This is why you never hand a raw request observable to code that might subscribe more than once.

How to spot it

The Network tab shows duplicate identical requests. If you see GET /me twice on one page load, count your async pipes.

How to say it in an interview

“HTTP observables are cold — the request fires on subscribe, so two async pipes mean two subscriptions and two calls. I'd either subscribe once in the template with *ngIf="user$ | async as user", or multicast with shareReplay(1) if multiple components need it. The scarier version is a POST — subscribing twice submits twice.”

question: What happens to a stream after it errors?
answer: An error is a terminal event — the observable stops and emits nothing further, and the subscription is torn down. To keep a long-lived stream alive (like a search box), handle the error inside the inner observable with catchError so the outer stream never sees it.
tip: Error and complete both end a stream permanently — design for that.
deep:
The contract

An observable's lifetime is: zero or more next, then exactly one of complete or error — never both, never anything after. Both are terminal: the subscription is torn down, teardown logic runs, and the stream can never emit again.

source$: --1--2--X
//                ^ error -- nothing can follow. Not a pause: an end.

Why this causes real bugs

// The search box that mysteriously stops working
this.searchTerm$.pipe(
  switchMap(t => this.api.search(t)),
  catchError(() => of([]))      // outer!
).subscribe(r => this.results = r);

The first failed request errors the outer stream. catchError handles it, emits [], and the outer stream completes. The user keeps typing and nothing ever happens again — no error in the console, no clue. Fix: catchError inside the switchMap, so the outer stream never sees an error.

A dead Subject stays dead

const sub = new Subject<number>();
sub.error('boom');
sub.next(1);           // silently ignored
sub.subscribe({ error: e => console.log(e) });  // immediately re-errors 'boom'

A Subject remembers it errored: every future subscriber is immediately handed the same error. This is why an errored NgRx-style state Subject bricks a feature until reload — and why you never let an error reach a long-lived Subject.

Complete is terminal too

this.destroy$.complete();
this.destroy$.next();   // no-op — completed streams ignore next

Same trap in reverse: completing a Subject you still intend to push through means later next() calls vanish silently.

Keeping a stream alive: catch on the inner

catchError positionResult
Outer pipeerror handled, outer stream ends
Inside switchMapinner dies, outer keeps going ✔

Same rule in NgRx effects: an uncaught error kills the effect permanently, so the inner catchError returning a failure action is mandatory.

retry re-subscribes rather than resurrects

retry doesn't revive the dead stream — it re-subscribes to the source, creating a fresh execution. That only works because the source is cold.

How to say it in an interview

“Error and complete are both terminal — one or the other, and then the stream is finished for good. The practical fallout is the search box that dies after one failed request: the catchError was on the outer stream, so it handled the error and completed everything. Catch inside the switchMap and the outer stream never sees it. And an errored Subject is permanently poisoned — it hands the same error to every new subscriber.”

question: How do Observables and Signals interoperate?
answer: (Angular 16+) toSignal(obs$) subscribes and exposes the latest value as a signal (auto-unsubscribes); toObservable(sig) turns a signal into a stream. Use RxJS for async/events, signals for synchronous local state, and bridge between them.
tip: toSignal replaces the async pipe in signal-based components and auto-cleans up.
deep:
The two bridges

// Observable -> Signal  (subscribes, auto-unsubscribes on destroy)
user = toSignal(this.http.get<User>('/me'));            // Signal<User | undefined>
count = toSignal(this.store.count$, { initialValue: 0 }); // Signal<number>

// Signal -> Observable  (emits on every signal change)
query = signal('');
results$ = toObservable(this.query).pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q))
);

toSignal's initial-value problem

A signal must always have a value, but an observable may not have emitted yet. Three ways to resolve it:

CallTypeBehaviour
toSignal(obs$)T | undefinedundefined until first emission
toSignal(obs$, { initialValue: 0 })Tyour default until first emission
toSignal(obs$, { requireSync: true })Tthrows unless it emits synchronously

requireSync: true is for sources that are guaranteed synchronous — a BehaviorSubject or an NgRx selector. Use it on an http.get and it throws immediately.

toSignal vs the async pipe

<!-- async pipe: subscribes PER USAGE -- 2 pipes = 2 HTTP calls -->
<span>{{ (user$ | async)?.name }}</span>
<img [src]="(user$ | async)?.avatar">

<!-- toSignal: ONE subscription, read as many times as you like -->
<span>{{ user()?.name }}</span>
<img [src]="user()?.avatar">

That's a real advantage: toSignal subscribes once in the class, so the multiple-async-pipe duplicate-request problem disappears. It's also readable in TypeScript (this.user()), which the async pipe never was.

toObservable is effect-based — the subtle bit

const s = signal(0);
const obs$ = toObservable(s);
s.set(1); s.set(2); s.set(3);   // obs$ emits only 3, not 1,2,3

toObservable is built on an effect, so it emits on the next microtask, not synchronously per set. Intermediate values are coalesced away. If you need every value, you needed a Subject, not a signal.

When to use which

  • Signals — synchronous local/derived UI state. No subscription, glitch-free computed, and they enable zoneless change detection.
  • RxJS — anything async or event-based: HTTP, WebSockets, debouncing, cancellation, retry, combining streams. Signals have no concept of time.

The mature line: signals didn't replace RxJS. Signals are for state, RxJS is for events over time — you convert at the boundary.

How to say it in an interview

“toSignal subscribes and exposes the latest value as a signal, auto-unsubscribing on destroy — and unlike the async pipe it subscribes once, so reading it twice in a template doesn't fire two HTTP calls. You have to handle the initial value, either with initialValue or requireSync for a synchronous source like a selector. toObservable goes the other way, but it's effect-based, so it coalesces intermediate values. I use signals for synchronous state and RxJS for async and event streams, converting at the boundary.”

question: When should you use tap?
answer: tap runs a side effect (logging, setting a flag, triggering an action) without altering the emitted value or the stream. Great for debugging and for effects that shouldn't change data. Never mutate values inside it.
tip: tap is for side effects only — if you're transforming the value, use map.
